event: Hunter-gatherer communities inhabit the coastal lowlands and river estuaries of present-day Guinea-Bissau during the Stone Age.
Iron-working and agricultural societies cultivating rice and other crops become established across the region.
The coastal region falls within the sphere of trans-Saharan trade networks linked to the Ghana Empire to the north.
The Mali Empire expands under Sundiata Keita, drawing the interior of the region into its tributary trade system.
The Kingdom of Kaabu emerges as a powerful Mandinka state, becoming a western province of the Mali Empire.
Portuguese navigator Nuno Tristão reaches the coast and rivers of the region, among the first Europeans to make contact.
Venetian explorer Alvise Cadamosto, sailing for Portugal, explores the Bijagós Islands and the Geba River estuary.
Portuguese and Cape Verdean traders establish trading posts (feitorias) along the rivers to deal in gold, ivory, and slaves.
Cacheu develops as a major Portuguese trading settlement and a hub of the transatlantic slave trade.
Portugal formally establishes a fortified captaincy at Cacheu to control trade on the Guinea coast.
The town of Bissau is founded as a Portuguese fortified trading post on the Geba estuary.
The Kingdom of Kaabu reaches its height as an independent Mandinka power after the decline of the Mali Empire.
Portugal constructs a fort at Bissau to strengthen its hold against rival European traders and local resistance.
Fula and Mandinka conflicts intensify as Islamic Fula states challenge the dominance of the Kingdom of Kaabu.
The Battle of Kansala marks the fall of the Kaabu Empire's capital to Fula forces, ending Kaabu's power.
Portuguese Guinea is administratively separated from Cape Verde and established as a distinct colony.
Franco-Portuguese conventions fix the borders of Portuguese Guinea with neighboring French colonies.
Portugal launches sustained 'pacification' military campaigns to subdue resistant interior peoples and the Bijagós.
Portuguese forces complete the conquest of the Bijagós Islands, ending decades of armed African resistance.
The colonial capital is moved from Bolama to Bissau, consolidating administration in the mainland port city.
Portugal reclassifies Portuguese Guinea as an 'overseas province' to deflect international decolonization pressure.
Amílcar Cabral and others found the PAIGC (African Party for the Independence of Guinea and Cape Verde).
Portuguese police kill striking dockworkers in the Pidjiguiti massacre, radicalizing the independence movement.
The PAIGC launches its armed war of independence against Portuguese colonial rule.
The PAIGC controls much of the countryside and establishes elected People's Assemblies in liberated zones.
PAIGC leader Amílcar Cabral is assassinated in Conakry in January, and the party unilaterally declares independence in September.
Following Portugal's Carnation Revolution, Portugal recognizes Guinea-Bissau's independence and the country joins the United Nations.
João Bernardo 'Nino' Vieira seizes power in a coup, ending plans for unification with Cape Verde.
A new constitution is adopted, formalizing one-party rule under the PAIGC with Vieira as head of state.
Constitutional reforms legalize opposition parties, opening the way to multiparty politics.
Guinea-Bissau holds its first multiparty presidential and legislative elections, won by Nino Vieira and the PAIGC.
Guinea-Bissau joins the West African CFA franc currency union, adopting the franc as its currency.
A military mutiny sparks a devastating civil war between rebel troops and forces loyal to President Vieira.
The civil war ends as President Vieira is ousted by the military and forced into exile.
Kumba Ialá of the PRS wins the presidency in elections following the end of the civil war.
A bloodless military coup overthrows President Kumba Ialá amid political paralysis and economic crisis.
Nino Vieira returns from exile and wins the presidential election, reclaiming power.
President Vieira is assassinated by soldiers, a day after the army chief of staff is killed in a bombing.
A military coup disrupts the presidential election runoff, plunging the country into renewed instability.
Elections restore civilian rule as José Mário Vaz wins the presidency, easing international sanctions.
Umaro Sissoco Embaló is inaugurated as president after a disputed election, deploying troops around state institutions.
President Embaló survives an attempted coup in which several people are killed during an attack on the government palace.
